tools.name: list_designs
tools.description: List designs available in this project (one per .sexp file in src/ containing a top-level design-block). Returns an array of objects with fields: name (file basename), title (human-readable design-block title), sections (array of top-level section names), instance_count, net_count, mtime (Unix seconds of last source modification), build_ok (false if the design failed to evaluate, in which case counts and sections are empty).

tools.name: list_library
tools.description: List names available in the project's lib/. Returns {components,modules,pinouts,footprints}, each entry with name + optional description. Omit query to list everything in directory order. Pass query to fuzzy-match (case-insensitive, over each entry's name + description): a contiguous substring scores highest, then an in-order subsequence, so partial names like "stm32" or "buck" return just the relevant entries ranked best-first instead of the whole library. Use the returned names in (import ...) forms and instance definitions.

query.description: Optional fuzzy filter applied to each entry's name + description across all four categories. Omit or leave blank to list everything.

tools.name: list_history
tools.description: List snapshot ids for a design, newest first. Each id is a timestamp usable with restore_version.

tools.name: list_instances
tools.description: List every instance in the design with its ref_des (auto-assigned), label (user-given name from source), component family, symbol, value, and pin count. Use this to bridge between source-side names (stm32, expansion) and board-side ref_des (U1, U8). `name` accepts a design or a lib/modules module name — modules resolve standalone via their parameter defaults (real instantiations are not consulted).

tools.name: list_free_pins
tools.description: List unassigned pins on an instance with their default pinout function names and a best-effort category (gpio|power|clock|analog|other). Use for picking GPIOs for new peripherals without scanning the full pinout file.

ref.description: Reference designator of the instance (e.g. U1)

tools.name: get_net
tools.description: Return every pin connection on a net plus any passive instances (R/L/C/F/D) on it. Use to audit decoupling or verify wiring without scanning the whole source. `name` accepts a design or a lib/modules module name — modules resolve standalone via their parameter defaults (real instantiations are not consulted).

tools.name: describe_component
tools.description: Describe a library component or component-family by name. Reads lib/components/<name>.sexp plus its referenced lib/pinouts/<ref>.sexp in one call. Returns {ok, name, kind: "component"|"component-family", is_family, description, footprint, pinout_ref, pinout_source, symbol_ref, manufacturer, mpn, datasheets[], pins[{id, function, alts[{name, kind}]}], requirements[{id, text, ref:{pdf, page, quote}|null, check_kind}]}. pinout_source is the path to the upstream .kicad_sym that produced the pinout (populated when the pinout was last regenerated via regenerate_pinout) — if a pin function name disagrees with the datasheet, edit that file in lib/sources/ then call regenerate_pinout. check_kind is the same primitive label the build-time review uses (decoupling, pullup-range, voltage-range, etc.) so you can see at a glance which requirements have a machine check vs are reviewer-judged. Use this before instantiating a part — pin function names from the pinout are what requirement checks reference, and the alts list shows alternate names if the pinout was merged with an alt-functions table. Returns {ok:false, error} if the component or pinout is missing.

name.description: Component basename (e.g. "adar2004accz", "cap-0402"). Same names list_library returns under "components".

tools.name: get_schematic
tools.description: Fetch the current scene graph JSON for a design. This is the same payload the browser viewer renders. `name` accepts a design or a lib/modules module name — modules resolve standalone via their parameter defaults (real instantiations are not consulted).

tools.name: get_pcb_layout_image
tools.description: Render a design's PCB layout (force-directed auto-placement) as a PNG image so you can VISUALLY inspect part arrangement, decoupling loops, airwires, and optionally routed copper — not just parse coordinate JSON. Returns an image content block. A plain call shows the whole board; pass nets and/or refs to enter focus mode: the named nets and components are spotlighted in amber while everything else is dimmed, for studying one subsystem in context. Airwire colours: orange=hot decoupling loop, cyan=ground return, grey=signal. With route=true it also overlays red=top-copper / blue=bottom-copper traces, vias, and red rings on DRC violations. Uses the design's saved auto-layout when present (set regen=true to force a fresh solve). The header always prints the objective breakdown (obj / hpwl / loop nH / compactness / congestion). Diagnostic overlays for figuring out what to improve: blame=true (cost heatmap + worst-offenders, the best starting view), loops=true (per-loop nH labels), dims=true (mm distances cap→pin), grid=true (mm reference grid), and compare=<saved-layout> (diff against another layout with movement arrows + Δobjective). Part labels follow names=ref|origin|both (spec-driven renders default to origin names, the vocabulary the (placement ...) spec uses). pins=["U13"] (or "hubs") labels pad net names on those parts. When a (placement ...) spec drove the solve, the header shows its coverage (SPEC: ALL PLACED / N UNPLACED / FELL BACK TO AUTO) and spec-unlisted parts are pin-hug AUTO-FILLED beside their placed pads (amber outline - usable, but the spec doesn't pin them); a part the solver couldn't safely place is hatched red in a staging band (NOT deliberately placed). Side lists accept (net "VIN") membership rules that expand to every unclaimed part on that net. Extra views: crop= zooms on one part (r= radius mm), sheet=true returns a contact sheet (board + per-hub closeups), critique=true draws a numbered worst-problems overlay.

name.description: Design name (as from list_designs), or a lib/modules module name.

nets.description: Net names to spotlight (case-insensitive). Also accepts a comma-separated string.

refs.description: Reference designators to spotlight, e.g. ["U1","C3"]. Also accepts a comma-separated string.

route.description: Run the autorouter and overlay copper traces, vias, and DRC violations (default false: placement + airwires only).

width.description: Output image width in pixels (default 1200; clamped).

layout.description: Render a specific saved layout by name instead of the auto layout.

sub.description: Scope the render to a single sub-block (slugified sub-block name).

regen.description: Force a fresh placement solve instead of using the cached auto-layout.

rough.description: Use the ROUGH seed instead of the force solve: each module is kept as an intact, grid-aligned rigid block (a flat module rings the anchor IC with each passive on the side of the pad it serves). A legible, module-coherent starting point, deliberately not metric-optimal; forces a fresh solve.

blame.description: Blame heatmap: tint each component by its share of the objective (green=cheap, red=expensive) and list the 3 worst-placed parts top-right. Best first view for deciding what to move.

loops.description: Label each decoupling loop with its connection inductance (nH); ribbon width scales with it.

dims.description: Draw labelled mm dimension leaders on each hot loop's power leg (cap-pad-to-IC-pin distance).

grid.description: Overlay a 1/2/5 mm reference grid with axis tick labels for absolute measurement.

compare.description: Saved-layout name to diff the current placement against: ghost outlines + movement arrows + a delta-objective in the score line.

names.description: Part-name labels: 'ref' = flattened ref-des (C150), 'origin' = the stable module-local name a (placement ...) spec is written in (C_BOOT1), 'both' = REF=ORIGIN. Default: origin when a (placement ...) spec drives the solve, ref otherwise.

pins.description: Label these parts' pads with their net names (VIN/SW/FB/GND) so pin functions are readable off the image. Case-insensitive refs (sub-block leaf ok); the token 'hubs' labels every hub IC. Also accepts a comma-separated string.

crop.description: Zoom: crop the viewport to a window around this part (ref-des, sub-block leaf, or origin name). Combine with pins= for a readable pin-level closeup.

sheet.description: Contact sheet: the whole board plus per-hub pin-labeled closeups in one image.

critique.description: Callout overlay: numbered markers + a fix-first panel (hottest loops by nH, longest airwire, staged parts, DRC count).

tools.name: describe_pcb_layout
tools.description: Structured spatial facts about a design's solved PCB placement - the textual twin of get_pcb_layout_image, built from the IDENTICAL placement so the facts always describe the board the image shows. Use the two together: the image for gestalt, this for measurements. Returns JSON with: axes convention (y grows down; 'top' = -y, matching (placement ...) spec words); anchor (the largest hub IC facts are oriented around); board w/h mm; score (objective/hpwl/loop_nh); parts[] each with ref + origin (the stable module-local name a (placement ...) spec uses) + kind/x/y/rot/size + side (left|right|top|bottom of the anchor) + gap_mm (courtyard clearance to the anchor) + nets[] on its pads + auto_filled flag (pin-hug placed, spec doesn't pin it) + unplaced flag (solver found no safe spot, still staged); loops[] (each decoupling loop's cap, hub, net, power-leg mm, inductance nH, and which side of the hub the cap sits on); hub_pads[] (per hub IC, each net's package edge(s) and pad count - e.g. VIN pads on the left edge); placement spec coverage (source spec|force, unplaced + auto_filled ref lists); and with route=true a routed summary (trace_mm/tracks/vias/drc). Accepts the same placement-selection args as the image tool.

name.description: Design name (as from list_designs), or a lib/modules module name.

route.description: Also route the board and include routed:{trace_mm,tracks,vias,drc}.

layout.description: Describe a specific saved layout by name instead of the auto layout.

sub.description: Scope to a single sub-block (slugified sub-block name).

regen.description: Force a fresh placement solve instead of the cached auto-layout.

rough.description: Describe the ROUGH seed instead of the force solve: each module kept as an intact, grid-aligned rigid block (a flat module rings the anchor IC with each passive on the side of the pad it serves). Deliberately not metric-optimal; forces a fresh solve.

tools.name: compare_layout_to_starred
tools.description: Score a design ?rough=1 auto-seed against its STARRED layout (the saved layout marked default/star in /pcb-layout - the hand-finished reference). Interchangeable-aware: each rough part is matched to the nearest starred part of the same class (kind + value + footprint + net set), so swapping which fungible 100nF cap sits on an edge is not penalized. Returns JSON {name, starred, n, area_match_pct (fraction of parts in the same general area - per interchangeable class, per IC edge, count agreement - the right-area headline; tolerant of looseness, only the wrong edge/proportion costs), classes[] each {class, n, matched, rough{per-edge counts}, starred{per-edge counts}} showing which subsystem the rough scattered onto different edges than the hand}. starred is null with a message when nothing is starred yet. Measures how hand-like the rough seed is / how little dragging remains to finish by hand, NOT the electrical score.

name.description: Design name (as from list_designs), or a lib/modules module name.

tools.name: set_part_poses
tools.description: Move parts on a design's working (or named) PCB layout and persist the poses into its <design>.layouts.json sidecar. Each pose is {ref, x_mm, y_mm, rot?, side?, locked?}: ref is a component ref-des (C3, buck/U1) or its module-local origin key (resolved exact-ref first, then origin key scoped by sub-block prefix); x_mm/y_mm are the part CENTRE in board mm (y grows DOWN, matching describe_pcb_layout); rot degrees CCW; side top|bottom (bottom mirrors + routes on B.Cu); locked true refuses viewer drag. Unlisted parts keep their poses; a moved part's nets' persisted copper is dropped (stale); an unknown ref fails the whole call without writing. Returns {ok, live_version, layout, total_parts, moved, routes_dropped, updated[]}.

name.description: Design name (as from list_designs) or a lib/modules module name.

poses.description: Parts to move: [{ref, x_mm, y_mm, rot?, side?, locked?}, ...].

layout.description: Target a named saved layout (module boards) instead of the working/starred one.

tools.name: set_board_outline
tools.description: Draw the working layout's board outline and persist it into <design>.layouts.json. Supply a rectangle {x,y,w,h} in mm (top-left origin, w/h positive) via rect, OR a closed polygon of >=3 [x,y] mm vertices via pts (its bbox fills the rect fields). The outline becomes the placement's board edge — drawn by every renderer, checked by the board-edge DRC, and profiled as the Gerber Edge.Cuts. Bootstraps a fresh auto placement when the design has no layout yet. Returns {ok, live_version, layout, outline}.

name.description: Design name or a lib/modules module name.

rect.description: Rectangle outline {x,y,w,h} in mm (w/h must be positive).

pts.description: Polygon outline as >=3 [x,y] mm vertex pairs.

layout.description: Target a named saved layout instead of the working/starred one.

tools.name: route_pcb
tools.description: Autoroute the working layout's current poses at the design's resolved routing rules (its (stackup ...) / (net-class ...) widths + clearances, baked into the placement) and persist the routed copper into the layout. Returns {ok, live_version, layout, routed, total, unrouted[], drc, trace_mm, tracks, vias, scope}. With nets, the router still runs full-board but only those nets' fresh copper is adopted (other nets keep their prior copper) — a full re-route is considered, so a scoped re-route can differ from routing that net in isolation.

name.description: Design name or a lib/modules module name.

nets.description: Restrict the persisted copper to these net names (others keep existing copper). Omit to route the whole board.

layout.description: Route a named saved layout instead of the working/starred one.

tools.name: save_pcb_layout
tools.description: Persist the current working state as a named saved layout, optionally starred (the star default that the KiCad sync + fab outputs read). A top-level design keeps exactly one auto-starred layout (star implied); modules / sub-circuits keep a multi-snapshot history saved under layout_name. Returns {ok, live_version, single, starred, layout}.

name.description: Design name or a lib/modules module name.

layout_name.description: Snapshot name for module boards (default "manual"). Ignored for single-layout designs.

star.description: Mark this the starred (default) layout, clearing any other star.

tools.name: clear_routes
tools.description: Drop persisted routed copper from the working layout — all of it, or only the given nets. Keeps poses, board outline, and silkscreen text. The inverse of route_pcb. Returns {ok, live_version, layout, cleared} (segment count removed).

name.description: Design name or a lib/modules module name.

nets.description: Only clear these net names' copper. Omit to clear all copper.

layout.description: Target a named saved layout instead of the working/starred one.

tools.name: run_fab_readiness
tools.description: The pre-fab correctness gate for the design's blessed (starred) or named layout: {ok, errors[], warnings[], stats{}} computed against the SAME fab view the Gerber export builds. Read-only — the identical gate on GET /api/pcb-gerbers returns 409 on errors. Run it after routing and before pulling Gerbers.

name.description: Design name or a lib/modules module name.

layout.description: Check a specific saved layout by name instead of the blessed one.

tools.name: get_version
tools.description: Return the current live version integer for a design. Increments on every successful build.

tools.name: run_checks
tools.description: Run ERC on a design or lib/modules module (module names resolve standalone via parameter defaults). Optional filters: severity ("error"|"warning"|"info", default all); changed_since (snapshot id from list_history — only ERC violations whose ref_des or net was added/removed since that snapshot are returned). Returns {filtered,changed_refs,changed_nets,erc:[...]}.

changed_since.description: Snapshot id to diff the current design against; ERC violations are filtered to those touching instances or nets that differ.

tools.name: get_language_reference
tools.description: The S-expression design-language reference, auto-generated live from the evaluator's own dispatch tables (identical content to docs/language-forms.md - it can never be stale). Covers every special form, builtin operator, (fmt ...) directive, SI numeric-literal suffix, design-scope form (with arity and allowed scopes), and section-name classifier keyword. Returns Markdown. Pass section to fetch a single '## ' section by title (case-insensitive); omit for the full document. An unknown title errors with the live list of valid section titles. Consult this before authoring or editing .sexp design/module files.

section.description: Optional section title, currently one of: Special forms | Builtin operators | String formatting directives | Numeric literals | Design-scope forms | Section-name classifier keywords. Omit for the whole reference.

tools.name: preview_module
tools.description: Evaluate a lib/modules (defmodule ...) standalone with caller-chosen arguments - module-level schematic design without a host board. Synthesizes a request-local wrapper design (nothing is written to disk) and returns view=summary (default): {ok,module,title,ports,instances,nets,sub_blocks,erc,assertions} - assertions surface the module's (assert / assert-range) design math; or view=scene_graph: the full schematic scene-graph JSON (same shape as get_schematic). A module's boundary (port ...) nets naturally look floating when evaluated in isolation - read ERC with that in mind. On eval failure returns {ok:false,error,diagnostic:{file,line,col,message,source_line}}. Module design loop: read/edit lib/modules/<m>.sexp with the VFS tools, preview here with the args you care about, iterate. For module-level LAYOUT, pass the module name directly as name to get_pcb_layout_image / describe_pcb_layout (they resolve module names via a real instantiation found in a design, else a zero-arg call).

args.description: Raw S-expression argument text for the module call, positional or named - e.g. "220k 47k 1k" or "(rfbt 220k) (rfbb 47k) (rled 1k)". Omit for parameter-less modules.

view.description: summary (default) = compact netlist + ERC + assertions; scene_graph = full schematic JSON.

tools.name: restore_version
tools.description: Restore a design to a prior snapshot. The current state is itself snapshotted first, so the restore is undoable.

tools.name: read_file
tools.description: Read a project file. Path is project-relative (e.g. "src/foo.sexp", "lib/components/cap-0402.sexp"). Text files come back in `content` with `binary:false`; binary files (.pdf, .step, .png, .jpg, .gif, .zip, .gz, .tar, .bin, .kicad_pcb_bin) come back in `base64` with `binary:true`. write_file is text-only, so PDFs and other binary files are read-only via MCP — the user adds them by dropping into lib/datasheets/ on disk or via the browser library page. Optional `offset` and `limit` window the response. Always returns the full file's `sha256` so you can pass it as `expected_sha256` on a follow-up write to defend against concurrent edits. Reads are sandboxed: files under auth/, oauth_clients.json, .git/, and similar credentials are denied. See resource eda://docs/workspace for the full readable/writable path table.

offset.description: Byte offset to start reading at (default 0)

limit.description: Max bytes to return (default = full file)

tools.name: list_dir
tools.description: List directory entries under `path` (project-relative; pass empty string for the project root). Each entry has {path, kind: file|dir|symlink|other, size, mtime}. Pass `recursive=true` to walk the subtree. `max_entries` caps the response (default 1024). Denied entries (auth/, oauth state) are silently filtered. Note: bare `lib` is not directly listable — use list_library or list_dir on a subdir like `lib/components/`.

tools.name: glob
tools.description: Find files matching a glob pattern. Supports `*` (any chars in segment), `?` (one char), and `**` (any depth). Optional `base` restricts the search to a subdirectory. Output paths are project-relative.

tools.name: write_file
tools.description: Atomically write `content` (text — UTF-8 passes through verbatim, including µ/°/± and other non-ASCII inside quoted strings) to a sandboxed path (writes go to a tmp file then rename, so a crash never leaves a half-written file). Writes are restricted to src/, lib/components/, lib/modules/, lib/pinouts/, lib/footprints/, lib/sources/, and blocks/. lib/datasheets/ is read-only via MCP — PDFs are added by hand on disk or via the browser library page. Refuses to write through symlinks or to .layout files (binary, regenerated by build). Optional `expected_sha256` enables CAS — pass the sha256 from a prior read to fail loudly if the file changed underneath you. Returns `dirty_designs` (designs whose import graph reaches the file) and `library_changes` (which library entity, if any, the write touched — populated even when no design imports the file yet).

expected_sha256.description: Optional CAS guard: write fails if the file's current sha256 differs

tools.name: edit_file
tools.description: Find `old_string` once in the file and replace it with `new_string`. Fails with `old_string not found` on zero matches and `ambiguous match` on multiple matches (unless `replace_all=true`). Same sandbox rules as write_file. Optional `expected_sha256` for CAS.

old_string.description: Exact text to find. Include enough surrounding context to make it unique.

replace_all.description: Replace every occurrence instead of failing on multi-match (default false)

tools.name: delete_file
tools.description: Delete a single file under the write sandbox. Refuses to delete directories or symlinks. Returns dirty_designs.

tools.name: move_file
tools.description: Rename `from` → `to`. Both paths are sandbox-checked under the write allowlist. Atomic on the same filesystem.

tools.name: build
tools.description: Re-evaluate `<name>.sexp`, resolve the BOM, render the schematic, run ERC, and bump the live version so browser viewers refresh. The previous state is auto-snapshotted (visible in list_history; recoverable via restore_version) so a build is undoable. This is the "push" step in the typical workflow: edit files via write_file/edit_file, then call build to make changes visible. Returns {ok, version, eval_ok, snapshot, error?, assertion_failures[], erc[]}. eval_ok=false means the .sexp didn't parse — the file stays on disk in its broken state until you fix it (same as `vim foo.sexp; netlisp build foo` locally).

tools.name: regenerate_pinout
tools.description: Re-run the KiCad → eda pinout converter (same code path as `netlisp convert-pinout` CLI). Reads `<source>` (must be under lib/sources/, e.g. "lib/sources/adar2004accz.kicad_sym"), generates the auto-generated pinout content, and writes it to lib/pinouts/<name>.sexp. The pinout's `;; source: …` header records the linkage so describe_component can later surface `pinout_source` for the next agent. If the upstream pin name is wrong (e.g. the datasheet says RFIN4+ but the .kicad_sym says REFIN4+, or pin 40 is named VPOS instead of VPOS5), edit the .kicad_sym in lib/sources/ first — that path is writable — then call this to regenerate. Output `name` defaults to the source basename minus .kicad_sym; pass `filter` for multi-symbol library files. Returns {ok:true, source, path, bytes_written, sha256, dirty_designs, library_changes} or {ok:false, error}.

source.description: Path to the .kicad_sym source file under lib/sources/

name.description: Optional output basename (defaults to source basename without .kicad_sym extension)

filter.description: Optional symbol-name filter for multi-symbol library files

tools.name: list_design_notes
tools.description: Return the per-design TODO list parsed from `<design>.notes.md`. Each task has an `id` (8-char hex, stable across renames), `text`, `created` (YYYY-MM-DD UTC), and `completed` (YYYY-MM-DD UTC when done, else null). `scratchpad` is any free-form text in the file that isn't a structured task line. Returns {tasks:[{id,text,created,completed}], scratchpad}.

tools.name: add_design_note
tools.description: Append a new open TODO to a design's `<design>.notes.md`. The note is stamped with today's UTC date and gets a fresh 8-char hex id. Use this to log ERC errors, follow-ups, or pin-mapping fixes that should be addressed in the next revision (e.g. "screen LED pins defined backwards, swap PE5 and PE6"). Pair with `complete_design_note` once the design has been updated. Returns {ok:true, task:{id,text,created,completed:null}}.

tools.name: complete_design_note
tools.description: Mark a TODO as done by stamping today's UTC date into its `completed` field. Idempotent: if the task is already done, the existing completion date is preserved. Returns {ok:true} or an error if no task has the given id.

tools.name: reopen_design_note
tools.description: Clear a TODO's completion date so it moves back to the open list. Useful when a fix turns out to be incomplete and the same task needs another revision. Returns {ok:true} or an error if no task has the given id.

tools.name: remove_design_note
tools.description: Delete a TODO from `<design>.notes.md`. The scratchpad section is left intact. Returns {ok:true} or an error if no task has the given id.

tools.name: list_component_requirements
tools.description: List the (requirement ...) design rules declared on a library component (lib/components/<name>.sexp). These are datasheet-derived rules attached to the part itself, so every design that instantiates the part inherits them — they are NOT per-design (for per-design follow-ups use add_design_note). Returns {ok:true, name, requirements:[{id, text, ref:{pdf,page,quote}|null, check_kind}]}. id is the requirement's stable identifier (an explicit (id ...) sub-form if present, else the Crc32 of the text) — pass it to remove_component_requirement or cite it in a (verifies (req "REF" id)) design sign-off. check_kind is the machine-check primitive (decoupling, voltage-range, connected, pullup-range, ...) or null for reviewer-judged rules. Returns {ok:false,error} if the component is missing.

name.description: Component basename, e.g. "stm32n657l0h3q". Same names list_library returns under "components".

tools.name: add_component_requirement
tools.description: Append a (requirement ...) form to a library component's lib/components/<name>.sexp. Records a datasheet-derived design rule ON THE PART (e.g. a pin that must be tied to a rail, a mandatory decoupling cap, an absolute-max voltage) so every design that uses the part inherits it and the build-time review can enforce it. WHAT QUALIFIES AS A REQUIREMENT: a rule a schematic can VIOLATE and a reviewer or check can flag — a supply rail outside its valid range, a missing mandatory pull-up or decoupling cap, a pin that must be tied to a specific net, an absolute-maximum that must not be exceeded. WHAT DOES NOT: capabilities and feature descriptions ('Fast-mode I2C up to 1 MHz', 'Schmitt-trigger inputs', 'bidirectional level translation'), typical or informational specs ('standby current 2µA typ', 'operating temperature -40..85C'), power-on or behavioral notes ('I/Os are high-Z at reset'), and restatements or duplicates of another requirement. None of those can be 'violated' by a wiring choice, so they are NOT requirements — if you want to record them, use add_design_note on the design instead. Litmus test before adding: 'could a schematic break this rule, and would the review catch the break?' If no, do not add it. Keep one rule per requirement (don't bundle two constraints into one line); datasheet scrapes tend to over-collect, so prune aggressively. The requirement gets a stable id derived from its text (Crc32), matching the id used by (verifies ...) sign-offs. Optionally cite the datasheet via ref_pdf/ref_page/ref_quote. Optionally attach a machine check by passing a complete (check ...) S-expression in `check`; it is validated by the same parser the build uses and rejected if unrecognized. Existing source formatting and comments are preserved. Returns {ok:true,id,text}; returns {ok:false,error} on a duplicate (same derived id), an unknown/malformed check, or a missing component. To edit a requirement, remove it and add the new text (the id is text-derived).

text.description: The requirement statement (prose). The stable id is derived from this text.

ref_pdf.description: Optional datasheet filename to cite, e.g. "an5967.pdf" (should already be under lib/datasheets/).

ref_quote.description: Optional verbatim datasheet snippet supporting the requirement.

check.description: Optional machine check as a complete S-expression, e.g. (check (connected (pin "PDR_ON") (pin "VDDA18_AON"))). Validated before writing. See a requirement's check_kind in describe_component / list_component_requirements for the available primitives.

tools.name: remove_component_requirement
tools.description: Delete a (requirement ...) form from a library component's lib/components/<name>.sexp, matched by its id (from list_component_requirements / describe_component) or by exact text. Removes the whole form and the line it sits on; if it was the last requirement, the component's closing paren is preserved. Other source formatting and comments are untouched. Supply either id (preferred) or text. Returns {ok:true,removed_id}; returns {ok:false,error} if nothing matches.

id.description: Requirement id to remove (preferred). Get it from list_component_requirements.

text.description: Exact requirement text to remove, as an alternative to id.

tools.name: read_datasheet
tools.description: Extract text from a datasheet PDF under lib/datasheets/ (e.g. "lm66100.pdf") via the system ps2ascii tool. The extraction is time- and size-bounded and cached to a <stem>.txt sidecar, so the first call may take a few seconds and later calls are instant. Returns {ok:true, name, total_bytes, offset, returned_bytes, truncated, content} or {ok:false, error}. The response is windowed: without a `limit` you get the first ~20000 bytes with truncated:true and total_bytes telling you the full size — page through the rest with `offset` (and a larger `limit` if you really want a bigger slice). A full datasheet is far too large to read in one blob; search it in windows.

offset.description: Byte offset into the extracted text to start at (default 0)

limit.description: Max bytes of text to return (default 20000)

tools.name: download_footprint
tools.description: Fetch a part's ECAD model from Component Search Engine (componentsearchengine.com) by part number and import it into the project library in one step. Searches the site, scrapes the SamacSys part id, downloads the model ZIP, then runs it through the same converter as the /library zip upload: writes lib/components/<name>.sexp (linking pinout + footprint + manufacturer/MPN), lib/pinouts/<name>.sexp, lib/footprints/<name>.sexp, and lib/models/<name>.step (when a 3D model is present), plus the raw KiCad sources under lib/sources/. Authentication: the model download uses HTTP Basic auth, so the server must have CSE_EMAIL + CSE_PASSWORD set (via .env or the environment; read server-side, never passed over MCP); if they are unset the call returns {ok:false}. After a successful import you can reference the new component by its returned `component` name in a design and `build` it. Returns {ok:true, part_name, manufacturer, samac_id, zip, zip_size, component, footprint, pinout, has_3d_model} on success, or {ok:false, stage:"download"|"import", error} on failure (e.g. part not found, rejected credentials, or a zip missing KiCad files).

part_number.description: Manufacturer part number to search for, e.g. "LMX2595RHAT" or "INA228AIDGSR".

manufacturer.description: Optional manufacturer name to disambiguate when multiple parts share a number, e.g. "Texas Instruments". Case-insensitive substring match.

tools.name: download_datasheet
tools.description: Fetch a part's datasheet PDF by part number and store it under lib/datasheets/, where read_datasheet can then extract its text. Tries two sources in order: (1) Component Search Engine (componentsearchengine.com) — tolerates non-exact matches by retrying relaxed variants, scrapes the datasheet link off the part page, downloads + validates the %PDF header; (2) DigiKey as a fallback when CSE has no datasheet on file — resolves the part via the DigiKey Product Information API, unwraps any manufacturer interstitial on its datasheet_url (e.g. TI's gotoUrl redirect), follows redirects, and validates %PDF. Authentication: source 1 needs none (the suggestion API + off-site PDF are public); source 2 needs DIGIKEY_CLIENT_ID + DIGIKEY_CLIENT_SECRET, read server-side, never over MCP. Returns {ok:true, source:"componentsearchengine"|"digikey", file, size, part, manufacturer, datasheet_url} on success. When BOTH sources fail, returns {ok:false, error, cse_error, digikey_error} naming why each one did.

part_number.description: Manufacturer part number to search for, e.g. "LM66100DCKR" or "INA228AIDGSR".

manufacturer.description: Optional manufacturer name to disambiguate when multiple parts share a number, e.g. "Texas Instruments". Case-insensitive substring match.

tools.name: search_components
tools.description: Search Component Search Engine (componentsearchengine.com) for parts by name/number and return candidate matches WITHOUT importing anything — the read-only counterpart to download_footprint/download_datasheet. The exact query is tried first; if it returns nothing, relaxed variants are tried, so partial or family numbers still match. Each result reports whether CSE has an ECAD model (has_model) and a datasheet (has_datasheet) on file. Typical flow: search_components to find the exact part_number + manufacturer, then call download_footprint (and/or download_datasheet) with that part_number to import it into the library. Authentication: none — the suggestion API is public, so this works with no CSE credentials configured. Returns {ok:true, query, count, results:[{part_number, manufacturer, has_model, has_datasheet}]} or {ok:false, error} (e.g. a network/Cloudflare failure). An empty results array with ok:true means CSE matched nothing.

query.description: Part number or search term, e.g. "INA228" or "W25Q128".

limit.description: Max results to return (default 10, capped at 50).

tools.name: resolve_mpn
tools.description: Resolve a fuzzy query — a vague description or a partial/orderable part number — to real MANUFACTURER part numbers via the DigiKey Product Information API v4, WITHOUT importing anything. This is the discovery step in front of Component Search Engine: DigiKey is good at 'description -> canonical MPN', then you feed a returned mpn to search_components / download_footprint / download_datasheet (which match on an MPN you already know), or to check_stock for the full per-packaging price ladder. Each result also carries a manufacturer DATASHEET URL (a second datasheet source besides CSE), the DigiKey product page URL, the DigiKey orderable part number, plus live stock (quantity_available), the single-unit price (unit_price), and lifecycle status (product_status: Active / Obsolete / Last Time Buy / …) — so a candidate's availability is visible without a second call. Authentication: the server must have DIGIKEY_CLIENT_ID and DIGIKEY_CLIENT_SECRET set (via .env or the environment; an OAuth2 client-credentials app registered at developer.digikey.com); optionally DIGIKEY_API_BASE to target the sandbox host. Credentials are read server-side and never passed over MCP. DigiKey's KeywordSearch is a keyword AND-match (NOT semantic), so query with terse parametric fragments like the DigiKey search box — value + package + function in a few keywords ("3.3V LDO 1A SOT-223", "current monitor i2c") — not full sentences. If a query returns nothing, the tool auto-relaxes by dropping trailing keywords one at a time and takes the first variant with hits, so over-specific queries degrade gracefully instead of returning empty. Returns {ok:true, query, count, results:[{mpn, manufacturer, description, datasheet_url, product_url, digikey_part_number, quantity_available, unit_price, product_status}]} (datasheet_url/product_url/digikey_part_number/unit_price/product_status may be null; quantity_available is 0 when out of stock) or {ok:false, error} (credentials unset, token rejected, or network failure). An empty results array with ok:true means DigiKey matched nothing even after relaxation.

query.description: Part number, or terse keyword fragments (value + package + function), e.g. "INA228" or "3.3V LDO 1A SOT-223". Avoid full sentences — keyword match, not semantic.

limit.description: Max results to return (default 10, capped at 50).

tools.name: check_stock
tools.description: Live DigiKey stock + pricing for a known manufacturer part number or orderable DigiKey number. The 'is it actually on the shelf, and what does qty-N cost' step after resolve_mpn hands you a candidate MPN. Resolves the query through the same DigiKey Product Information API v4 keyword search as resolve_mpn (so a partial/fuzzy number still matches, auto-relaxing trailing keywords), then returns the FULL per-packaging detail each match omits in resolve_mpn: every orderable packaging variation (Cut Tape, Tape & Reel, Tube, …) with its own live quantity_available, minimum_order_quantity, and the complete quantity price-break ladder (break_quantity / unit_price / total_price). Use this to confirm a part is genuinely stocked and to read real volume pricing — not just a 1-off price. Authentication is identical to resolve_mpn: the server must have DIGIKEY_CLIENT_ID and DIGIKEY_CLIENT_SECRET set (via .env or the environment; an OAuth2 client-credentials app from developer.digikey.com), optionally DIGIKEY_API_BASE for the sandbox host; credentials are read server-side and never passed over MCP. Prices are in the catalog currency DigiKey returns for the account's locale. Returns {ok:true, query, count, results:[{mpn, manufacturer, description, product_status, quantity_available, unit_price, product_url, digikey_part_number, variations:[{digikey_part_number, package_type, quantity_available, minimum_order_quantity, price_breaks:[{break_quantity, unit_price, total_price}]}]}]} or {ok:false, error}. quantity_available is 0 when out of stock; an empty variations or price_breaks array means DigiKey published none (e.g. a marketplace listing). An empty results array with ok:true means nothing matched even after relaxation.

query.description: Manufacturer part number or DigiKey orderable number, e.g. "INA228AIDGSR" or "296-INA228-ND". A partial number works (keyword match) but an exact MPN gives the cleanest single match.

limit.description: Max matching products to return (default 5, capped at 50). Each result carries full price ladders, so keep this small.
